query: clinical presentation of acute kidney injury due to alcohol abuse and antifreeze ingestion

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Urinalysis : Urinalysis is utilized to check for ketones, as patients may have associated alcoholic ketoacidosis. Ketonuria without glycosuria can exclude alcoholic ketoacidosis and the ingestion of isopropyl alcohol. Myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis may be suspected when hematuria is noted on urinalysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In general, the prognosis for a patient presenting with AKA is good as long as the condition is identified and treated early. Delayed presentation or diagnosis may result in end-organ damage such as acute renal failure with tubular necrosis. [12] The long-term prognosis of patients diagnosed with AKA depends on the severity of their underlying alcohol abuse disorder rather than AKA itself. The primary cause of morbidity and mortality in patients diagnosed with AKA is under-recognition of concomitant diseases (that may have precipitated the AKA, to begin with). These include acute pancreatitis, gastrointestinal bleeding, and alcohol withdrawal. Mortality specifically due to AKA has been linked to the severity of serum beta-hydroxybutyric acid in some studies. It should be used as an indicator of the disease's severity. [13] Identifying these high-risk patients can help determine the intensity of monitoring required for each patient to ensure optimal outcomes are achieved.

retrieved_docs.title: Antidotes -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment and Antidotes
retrieved_docs.content: While both ethanol and fomepizole can are viable options in toxic alcohol poisoning, fomepizole has become the treatment of choice in the United States since its approval in 1997. It is a potent competitive inhibitor of alcohol dehydrogenase, has few side effects, is easy to use, and may preclude the need for hemodialysis in many patients. [36] Evidence for its use is primarily based on three retrospective case series and two prospective clinical trials. Recommended dosing of fomepizole is a loading dose of 15mg/kg followed by maintenance doses of 10mg/kg every 12 hours for two to four doses followed by 15mg/kg every 12 hours for the remaining doses. If fomepizole is unavailable IV or oral ethanol is an option. For 5% ethanol, the IV dose is 15ml/kg loading dose with infusion rate dependent on whether or not the patient is a regular drinker (4 to 8 ml/kg/hr) or not (2 to 4 ml/kg/hr). The dose for 10% IV ethanol is half that of the 5%. The doses are similar for oral preparations but decrease with increasing potency. For example, the loading dose for 20% ethanol is 4ml/kg and 2ml/kg for 40% ethanol. Maintenance doses are again dependent on the concentration of the ethanol, whether the patient is a regular drinker, and whether or not they are receiving hemodialysis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Ethylene Glycol Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History is often challenging to acquire in an intentional self-harm attempt or substance abuse scenario, and a physical exam can often be normal early in the course. Many patients may be embarrassed or may not want to admit to their actions. Commonly, patients underestimate the magnitude and severity of their ingestion. Accidental ingestions are often self-reported or witnessed. Many times, there is a diagnostic dilemma, and it is up to the clinician to consider toxic alcohol exposure as an etiology for a finding, such as metabolic acidosis with an elevated anion gap or, in this case, potentially with renal injury. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperoxaluria -- Etiology -- Other causes
retrieved_docs.content: Ethylene glycol, found in antifreeze, is commonly ingested accidentally or intentionally. In 2020, US poison control centers noted 6036 calls regarding ethylene glycol ingestion. Of these, 586 had at least moderate clinical effects, 30 of which resulted in death. Ethylene glycol is metabolized through alcohol dehydrogenase; an end product is oxalate. Oxalate crystals are often found in the urine of patients with ethylene glycol ingestion. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol -- Mechanism of Action -- Antidote
retrieved_docs.content: Ethylene glycol poisoning: Similar to methanol, ethylene glycol is metabolized by ADH in the liver to form glycolaldehyde, which ALDH converts to glycolic acid. Glycolic acid may be further metabolized into oxalic acid. Glycolic acid is more toxic than ethylene glycol, and oxalic acid can form calcium oxalate crystals in the kidney. Ethanol also has a greater affinity for ADH and ADLH than ethylene glycol and competitively inhibits ethylene glycol metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Antidotes -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment and Antidotes
retrieved_docs.content: Serum ethylene glycol concentration  greater than or equal to 62 mg/dL Methanol concentration greater than or equal to 32mg/dL Recent ingestion with an osmolal gap higher than 25 Suspected ingestion plus two or more of the following: arterial pH less than 7.3 serum bicarb less than 20 an osmolol gap greater than 25 presence of urinary oxalate crystals or visual disturbance

retrieved_docs.title: Acetaminophen Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- More than Eight Hours After Ingestion
retrieved_docs.content: Dialysis may be necessary if the patient develops acute kidney injury. Indications for dialysis include refractory fluid overload, severe hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, and uremia. Dose adjustments for patients with alcohol use disorder or those who are chronically ill are not required. Dialysis is considered safe for use during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ethanol Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for ethanol toxicity depends on multiple factors, including chronicity of use, degree of intoxication, associated traumatic injuries, and end-organ damage. Patients who have uncomplicated ethanol toxicity have a good prognosis and need to be counseled on abstinence. Most of the chronic complications that can develop from ethanol toxicity can be helped and sometimes reversed with alcohol abstinence. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Urinalysis - Urinalysis may reveal an elevated specific gravity, as the patient is typically dehydrated. The specific gravity of the urine can be within the normal range if the patient has been drinking recently enough for the effect of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) inhibition to still be present, or if the patient has been able to tolerate oral fluids over the last 24 hours, but not significant solid food.

retrieved_docs.title: Methanol Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient presents later in the clinical course, the osmolar gap may be normal. An observation period is unnecessary if the patient is already acidotic. However, if the patient is stable, serum or urine ketones should be checked, and treatment should include 1 to 2 L of isotonic, dextrose-containing intravenous fluids along with thiamine supplementation. Many patients at risk for toxic alcohol poisoning may also have alcoholic ketoacidosis or starvation ketosis. If improvement occurs, as evidenced by a decrease in acidosis and the anion gap, toxic alcohol ingestion becomes less likely, and other potential causes should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of alcoholic ketoacidosis includes all causes of high anion gap metabolic acidosis, andthey  are as follows: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) Methanol toxicity Ethylene glycol toxicity Uremia Lactic acidosis Salicylate poisoning Other differential diagnoses and often concomitantly present diseases that precipitate alcohol ketoacidosis in patients include: Pancreatitis Peptic ulcer disease Acute cholecystitis Acute mesenteric ischemia Alcohol withdrawal

retrieved_docs.title: Fibric Acid Antilipemic Agents -- Toxicity -- Managing Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Fibric acids are highly protein-bound, which rules out the use of hemodialysis. Managing an overdose of fibric acids is primarily symptomatic. Gastric lavage may be used in cases of overdose, provided the usual precautions for airway maintenance are in place. [45] If rhabdomyolysis is suspected during fibrate therapy, the medication should be discontinued immediately. Supportive care includes aggressive intravenous resuscitation with isotonic saline to preserve renal function and promote myoglobin clearance. Nephrology consultation is required for oliguric renal failure or rising azotemia. Urinary alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate remains a valuable treatment option in severe metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia cases. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 26-Year-Old Male Presenting With New Onset Seizure and History of PTSD (Archived) -- Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis has been effective in severe, life-threatening caffeine ingestions. [32] [3] [33] Caffeine exhibits ideal characteristics to be dialyzed, including low protein binding (36%), low molecular size (194), and a small volume of distribution (0.6 to 0.8 L/kg). [11] Caffeine is among several toxins that can be dialyzed, including isopropanol, salicylates, theophylline, uremia, methanol, barbiturates, beta-blockers, lithium, and ethylene glycol. Imminent cardiac arrest in caffeine toxicity should prompt intravenous lipid emulsion therapy [34] [33] to scavenge the free-serum caffeine.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications associated with AKA can occur with delayed diagnosis or under-resuscitation and failure to end ketogenesis. These include hypovolemic shock and cardiac arrest. Cardiac arrest from AKA is uncommon. It is essential to manage other electrolyte disturbances and alcohol withdrawal to prevent seizures and the dreaded delirium tremens. Some patients with chronic alcohol abuse disorder will have non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, which may present as pulmonary edema on fluid resuscitation to treat AKA. Adequate and frequent clinical as well as biochemical monitoring is required to identify these potential complications early and prevent the morbidity associated with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Antidotes -- Issues of Concern -- Background and Presentation
retrieved_docs.content: Ethylene glycol and methanol : Poisoning with ethylene glycol or methanol may occur through attempted self-harm, unintentional ingestion, or a misguided attempt at inebriation. In 2007, United States poison centers received a combined 8014 calls related to possible ethylene glycol or methanol exposure. [11] Ethylene glycol, an essential component of antifreeze, has a bright color and sweet taste, making it particularly appealing to children. Classic signs and symptoms of ethylene glycol poisoning include altered mental state, metabolic acidosis, acute renal failure, and oxalate crystals in the urine. Hypocalcemia, multiorgan dysfunction, and even death occur in severe cases. [11] Methanol is present in embalming fluids, paint removers, windshield-washer fluid, canned-heating products, and moonshine liquor. Methanol poisoning causes profound metabolic acidosis and visual changes that may lead to blindness. Severe cases can result in multiorgan failure and death. Untreated methanol poisoning carries a mortality rate as high as 28% with permanent visual defects or blindness in up to 30% of survivors. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: التجفاف (dehydration) and حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal psychological state) are clinically associated with فشل كلوي حاد (acute kidney failure). Dehydration can reduce renal perfusion and precipitate acute renal injury, while an abnormal psychological condition may contribute to the development or worsening of acute kidney failure through mechanisms such as medication misuse, poor self‑care, or stress‑related hemodynamic changes. Recognizing these associations helps clinicians identify at‑risk patients early, implement preventive measures, and tailor management strategies for those presenting with dehydration or mental health disturbances to reduce the likelihood of acute kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2591
retrieved_docs.content: The condition إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is clinically associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption); alcohol use increases the likelihood of sustaining a closed head injury, and patients presenting with such injuries often have a history of recent alcohol intake. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians when assessing trauma patients, guiding both acute management and preventive counseling regarding alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol intoxication (تَشَمُّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is clinically associated with hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), indicating that patients who present with severe alcohol‑related toxicity are at risk for elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting the common metabolic disturbance in which excess potassium and a lowered blood pH are observed together. Recognizing these interconnections is important for clinicians, as the presence of alcohol intoxication should prompt evaluation for hyperkalemia, and the detection of hyperkalemia should lead to assessment for accompanying acidosis to guide appropriate treatment and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with dehydration (التجفاف) and with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). When renal function declines sharply, the condition can give rise to extrarenal blood nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), reflecting the buildup of nitrogenous waste products that the kidneys are no longer able to clear. These interrelated clinical features highlight the importance of recognizing fluid deficits and neuropsychiatric changes as potential indicators of acute kidney injury and of monitoring nitrogen waste levels to guide timely management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 577
retrieved_docs.content: toxic alcohol exposure can intersect with fluid‑electrolyte disturbances. Overall, the interconnections among diabetes, hyperosmolar states, renal failure, acid‑base and electrolyte abnormalities, gastrointestinal secretion disorders, and alcohol‑related toxicity underscore the need for comprehensive, integrated assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration, abnormal psychological state, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes are clinically associated with acute kidney failure. Acute kidney failure often develops in the context of severe dehydration, which can precipitate rapid loss of renal function. Patients with acute kidney failure may also present with abnormal psychological states, reflecting the systemic impact of renal dysfunction on mental status. Moreover, type 2 diabetes that requires insulin therapy is linked to acute kidney failure, indicating that poorly controlled diabetes is a significant comorbidity that can predispose to or worsen renal injury. Recognizing these interconnections aids in comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) leads to increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما). Both dehydration and high plasma osmolality are clinically associated with acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney injury is considered a specific manifestation within urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), which in turn falls under the broader category of high plasma osmolality. Acute kidney injury is also linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم) and to type 2 diabetes requiring insulin (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). High plasma osmolality co‑occurs with low blood volume and is clinically associated with the same insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetes as well as with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). Abnormal mental states are additionally directly associated with acute kidney injury. Thus, dehydration, elevated plasma osmolality, reduced intravascular volume, insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetes, and neuropsychiatric disturbances are interrelated risk factors and clinical correlates of acute kidney injury within the spectrum of urinary tract disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول). In patients who consume alcohol, clinicians should consider that pulmonary imaging may reveal atypical or pathological changes, which can stem from alcohol‑related lung injury, aspiration events, infections, or other respiratory complications. Recognizing this association prompts healthcare providers to inquire about alcohol use when interpreting abnormal chest radiographs, to assess for potential alcohol‑related pulmonary disease, and to incorporate appropriate counseling or treatment for alcohol misuse into the overall patient care plan.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1758
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney injury is also clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم) and to abnormal psychological states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). Urinary tract disorder (اضطراب السبيل البولي) is a broader category that encompasses acute kidney injury, and high plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is a subcategory of urinary tract disorder. High plasma osmolality originates from dehydration, co‑occurs with low blood volume, and is clinically associated with abnormal psychological states. Consequently, dehydration can raise plasma osmolality, which together with hypovolemia may contribute to the development of acute kidney injury and related urinary tract disturbances, and both the physiological derangements and the renal injury are linked to psychological abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات). Hyperglycemia/new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد) is placed within the dehydration group and includes polyuria (تأثير إدرار البول) as a characteristic feature. Overall, the knowledge base underscores the interdependence of lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, electrolyte and acid‑base homeostasis, endocrine‑pancreatic function, renal health, respiratory and hepatic pathology, and substance‑use effects, highlighting the necessity of integrated assessment and multidisciplinary management for patients presenting with these overlapping conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with these forms of diabetes are at increased risk for developing sudden renal impairment. Dehydration (التجفاف) is also clinically linked to acute kidney failure, serving as a common precipitating factor that can exacerbate renal dysfunction in diabetic individuals. Recognizing the interplay among acute kidney failure, diabetes (both type 1 and insulin‑dependent type 2), and dehydration is essential for early identification, prevention, and management of renal complications in these patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: and hyperkalemia; its severity is heightened in the setting of type 2 diabetes and metformin use, which can precipitate hypoglycemia (low glucose) classified among other endocrine disorders. Juvenile nephropathic cystinosis, a rare inherited disorder classified under “other nutritional, endocrine, and metabolic disorders,” is related to amino‑acid metabolism disorders, inversely related to broader metabolic disorders, and exemplifies the connection between genetic metabolic defects and renal disease (cystinosis of the kidney). Carbohydrate‑metabolism disorders are a subset of metabolic disorders and intersect with other hormonal disorders, highlighting the integrated network of nutrient‑metabolism, renal, and endocrine dysfunctions that must be evaluated comprehensively in patient care. Alcoholic hepatitis and alcoholic intoxication illustrate hepatic contributions to systemic fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance; intoxication is associated with hyperkalemia and hepatic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.
